DBMS.a: The process of organizing tables to reduce data redundancy and avoid update/insert/delete anomalies, done in stages called normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF).

DBMS.q: What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?
DBMS.a: DELETE removes rows one at a time and can be rolled back; TRUNCATE removes all rows at once and resets identity, minimal logging; DROP removes the entire table structure from the database.

DBMS.q: What is a deadlock and how is it prevented?
DBMS.a: A deadlock occurs when two or more transactions wait on locks held by each other. Prevention strategies include lock ordering, timeouts, and wait-die/wound-wait schemes.

DBMS.q: What is the difference between a clustered and non-clustered index?
DBMS.a: A clustered index defines the physical order of rows on disk (one per table); a non-clustered index is a separate structure with pointers back to the row (multiple allowed).
Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — the guarantees a database transaction provides to keep data reliable even under failures or concurrent access.

DBMS.q: What is the difference between OLTP and OLAP?
DBMS.a: OLTP systems handle many short, frequent transactions (e.g. order processing); OLAP systems handle complex analytical queries over large historical datasets (e.g. reporting).

OS.q: What is a deadlock in operating systems, and what are its four necessary conditions?
OS.a: A deadlock is a state where processes wait indefinitely for resources held by each other. Conditions: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait.

OS.q: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
OS.a: A process is an independent program with its own memory space; a thread is a lightweight unit of execution within a process that shares memory with other threads of the same process.

OS.q: What is paging and why is it used?
OS.a: Paging divides memory into fixed-size blocks (pages/frames) to avoid external fragmentation and allow a process's logical address space to be non-contiguous in physical memory.
A synchronization primitive that uses a counter to control access to a shared resource by multiple processes/threads, preventing race conditions.
A condition where the system spends more time swapping pages in and out of memory than executing actual processes, usually due to insufficient physical memory for the current workload.

OS.q: Difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling?
OS.a: In preemptive scheduling the OS can forcibly suspend a running process to run another (e.g. Round Robin); in non-preemptive scheduling a process keeps the CPU until it finishes or blocks (e.g. FCFS).

CN.q: What happens when you type a URL into a browser?
CN.a: DNS resolves the domain to an IP, a TCP connection is established (with TLS handshake for HTTPS), the browser sends an HTTP request, the server responds, and the browser renders the page.

CN.q: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?
CN.a: TCP is connection-oriented, reliable, and ordered (with acknowledgements and retransmission); UDP is connectionless, faster, and does not guarantee delivery or order.
The TCP connection setup sequence: SYN from client, SYN-ACK from server, ACK from client — establishing a reliable connection before data transfer.

CN.q: What is the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
CN.a: HTTPS is HTTP layered over TLS/SSL, encrypting data in transit and verifying server identity via certificates; HTTP sends data in plain text.
Dividing a larger network into smaller logical sub-networks to improve routing efficiency, security, and address utilization.
A conceptual 7-layer model (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application) describing how data moves across a network.

OOPs.q: What are the four pillars of OOP?
OOPs.a: Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and Polymorphism — the core principles for structuring reusable, maintainable object-oriented code.

OOPs.q: What is the difference between method overloading and overriding?
OOPs.a: Overloading is defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameters within a class (compile-time); overriding is redefining a parent class's method in a subclass (runtime).

OOPs.q: What is polymorphism? Give a real example.
OOPs.a: The ability of an object to take multiple forms. Example: a `Shape` base class with `area()` overridden differently by `Circle` and `Rectangle` subclasses.

OOPs.q: What is an abstract class vs an interface?
OOPs.a: An abstract class can have both implemented and unimplemented methods and holds state; an interface (traditionally) only declares method signatures and supports multiple inheritance of type.
Defining multiple constructors in a class with different parameter lists so objects can be created in different ways.

OOPs.q: What is the diamond problem and how do languages resolve it?
OOPs.a: It arises when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from a common base, causing ambiguity. Java avoids it by disallowing multiple class inheritance and using interfaces; C++ resolves it via virtual inheritance.
